danger_signals.visual_form: Yellow flag flashing in top-left corner of screen
danger_signals.meaning: Crash or incident on track; speed restrictions imposed; passing forbidden until cleared

danger_signals.visual_form: Disabled vehicle remains visible on circuit for 2-3 laps
danger_signals.meaning: Active hazard on track; collision risk; yellow flag conditions active

danger_signals.visual_form: Car retired to pit lane; displayed in standings as 'Crashed' or specific failure type
danger_signals.meaning: Vehicle permanently out of race; loss of competitor
Imminent engine failure if revs continue unchecked

safety_signals.visual_form: Green flag display as leader exits turn 4
safety_signals.meaning: Track cleared; normal racing resumed; passing permitted; full speed allowed (~230 mph)

safety_signals.visual_form: No yellow flags; no wreckage visible; normal lap timing displays
safety_signals.meaning: No active incidents; safe to race at full competitive pace

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: 32 computer-controlled cars in various liveries matching 1989 Indianapolis 500 grid
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Always displayed in UI; tracked separately in standings from computer opponents

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Car numbers 1-33 (excluding 17); shows 'Crashed' or mechanical failure status in standings
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Realistic Indy car designs from 1989 season; visual damage states visible on track
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Displayed in standings screen with position, status, and retirement reason if applicable

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Green flag display; clear track; normal lap counter; cars maintain high speed (~230 mph)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: All cars race at full competitive pace; passing permitted; no speed restrictions

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Yellow flag flashing in top-left corner; wreckage visible on circuit; speed indicator reduced
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: All cars reduce speed to ~90 mph; passing forbidden; AI cars maintain formation until cleared

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Crashed car wreckage remains on track for 2-3 laps; reflected in standings as 'Crashed' status
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Affected computer car stops racing; other cars navigate around wreckage; yellow flag conditions persist

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Computer car in pits; standings show specific failure type (Engine, Bearing, Clutch, etc.)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Affected car leaves race permanently; removed from active competition display

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Wheel damage affects visual handling feedback; engine damage may affect speed output in replay mode
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Car becomes harder to control; performance degraded; recovery to pit increasingly difficult with multiple damage types

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: End-sequence celebration images displayed; final standings show player position (1st for victory); position ribbon at top of screen
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Return to main menu; end replay available; victory ceremony shown if first place achieved

environmental_storytelling.example: Computer cars show mechanical failure reasons (Radiator, Oil Leak, Valve, etc.) rather than generic 'DNF', providing specific failure narratives for each AI competitor
Crashed cars remain on circuit for 2-3 laps before being cleared, allowing player to witness consequence of incident over time and plan alternate racing lines

environmental_storytelling.technique: Yellow flag UI placement and persistence
environmental_storytelling.visual_form: Yellow flag flashing in top-left corner maintains visibility throughout caution period, creating urgency and hazard awareness
environmental_storytelling.example: Flag persists until 'green flag' display returns, teaching player to monitor top-left corner for state changes
Starting grid matches 1989 Indianapolis 500 qualifying order (with player replacing Car #29), grounding simulation in historical race context
Leader/Crash camera automatically focuses on incident vehicles during replay, directing player attention to consequential race moments

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Maintains racing line; competes at full speed; responds to yellow flag by reducing speed to ~90 mph
Becomes immobilized on track; wreckage remains visible for 2-3 laps; removed from race standings as 'Crashed'
Pulls into pits; remains there permanently; retirement reason displayed in standings (Engine, Bearing, Clutch, etc.)
Maintains drivability; handling becomes more difficult; recovery to pits still possible
Control severely degraded; recovery to pits extremely difficult or impossible; race effectively over
No recovery possible; race ended; mechanics cannot repair mid-race
Forced speed reduction to ~90 mph; passing forbidden; maintains formation until green flag; speeds return to ~230 mph upon clearance

notes: Indianapolis 500: The Simulation operates as a pure racing simulation with minimal narrative or faction elements. Visual communication focuses entirely on race-state signaling (green/yellow flags), damage consequences, and historical accuracy (1989 grid). No political situations, narrative factions, or world-state beyond immediate racing conditions are present. The game's manifestation layer is purely mechanical: flags signal rule changes, wreckage signals hazards, and standings display communicate competitor elimination. Color identity (yellow/red/blue cars) is cosmetic rather than faction-driven.
